---
title: "Projects"
---

<div class="project-detail-page">

<h1 class="project-detail-title">Projects</h1>

<div class="project-detail-card" id="short-dated-options-volatility-forecasting-and-trading-strategy">

<h2>Short-Dated Options Volatility Forecasting and Trading Strategy</h2>

<div class="project-tags">
<span>Volatility Forecasting</span>
<span>Options Trading</span>
<span>GARCH</span>
<span>LSTM</span>
<span>Neural Networks</span>
</div>

<p>
This project develops an end-to-end framework that connects short-horizon volatility forecasting with systematic SPY options strategy design. I modeled annualized 5-day realized volatility using <strong>GARCH-family</strong> models, <strong>Random Forests</strong>, feedforward <strong>neural networks</strong>, and <strong>LSTM</strong>-based models, then compared the forecasts with at-the-money implied volatility to construct a volatility-edge signal.
</p>

<p>
The strategy component translates this signal into long- and short-volatility option positions, with attention to option structure selection, entry and exit rules, position sizing, and risk management. The full workflow was implemented and backtested in <strong>QuantConnect</strong>.
</p>

<p><strong>Methods:</strong> GARCH, EGARCH, GJR-GARCH, Random Forest, feedforward neural networks, LSTM, volatility-edge signal construction, options strategy design, backtesting</p>

<p><strong>Tools:</strong> Python, QuantConnect, scikit-learn, arch, pandas</p> 

<a class="project-link-button" href="files/volatility_forecasting_options_strategy.pdf">Read Paper</a>

</div>

<div class="project-detail-card" id="chess-anomaly-detection-with-metropolis-hastings-sampling">

<h2>Chess Anomaly Detection with Metropolis-Hastings Sampling</h2>

<div class="project-tags">
<span>Anomaly Detection</span>
<span>Metropolis-Hastings</span>
<span>Bayesian Computation</span>
<span>FastAPI</span>
<span>Stockfish</span>
</div>

<p>
This project develops a <strong>probabilistic anomaly detection framework</strong> for identifying unusually strong chess play by comparing observed move sequences against a counterfactual human baseline. The method uses <strong>Maia-based human move proposals</strong> and <strong>Stockfish-based centipawn-loss scoring</strong> within a <strong>Metropolis-Hastings sampling</strong> framework.
</p>

<p>
The project also includes a <strong>FastAPI service</strong> to make the sampler easier to run, reproduce, and test through structured API endpoints.
</p>

<p><strong>Methods:</strong> Metropolis-Hastings sampling, empirical p-value testing, Markov chain diagnostics</p>

<p><strong>Tools:</strong> Python, FastAPI, Docker, Stockfish, Polars</p>

</div>

<div class="project-detail-card" id="web-scraped-recipe-dataset-construction-and-interactive-analysis">

<h2>Web-Scraped Recipe Dataset Construction and Interactive Analysis</h2>

<div class="project-tags">
<span>Web Scraping</span>
<span>R Shiny</span>
<span>Data Cleaning</span>
<span>Interactive Analysis</span>
</div>

<p>
This project builds an automated <strong>web-scraping pipeline</strong> to collect and structure recipe data from the official game Wiki for <em>Stardew Valley</em>. The pipeline extracts recipe structure, ingredient composition, and numerical buff effects from heterogeneous HTML pages.
</p>

<p>
The final output includes an <strong>interactive R Shiny application</strong> that supports recipe search, craftable recipe identification, and minimal-additional-ingredient recommendations.
</p>

<p><strong>Methods:</strong> web scraping, HTML parsing, rule-based filtering, interactive application development</p>

<p><strong>Tools:</strong> R, R Shiny, rvest, dplyr, stringr</p>

<a class="project-link-button" href="https://github.com/SimengWu17/Stardew_Valley_R_Shiny">GitHub Repository</a>

</div>

<div class="project-detail-card" id="insurance-subrogation-outcome-prediction">

<h2>Insurance Subrogation Outcome Prediction</h2>

<div class="project-tags">
<span>Supervised Learning</span>
<span>Insurance Analytics</span>
<span>Classification</span>
<span>Model Evaluation</span>
</div>

<p>
This project focuses on predicting <strong>insurance subrogation outcomes</strong> using claim-level information, loss severity indicators, and recovery-related attributes. I performed preprocessing and feature engineering, including categorical encoding, interaction terms, and distributional transformations.
</p>

<p>
I developed and compared multiple tree-based models, including <strong>XGBoost</strong>, <strong>LightGBM</strong>, <strong>CatBoost</strong>, and <strong>TabM</strong>. The modeling pipeline emphasized reproducible experimentation, cross-validation, and out-of-sample robustness.
</p>

<p><strong>Methods:</strong> classification, feature engineering, cross-validation, model comparison, ensemble learning</p>

<p><strong>Tools:</strong> Python, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, scikit-learn, SHAP</p>

</div>

<div class="project-detail-card" id="momentum-based-algorithmic-trading-strategy">

<h2>Momentum-Based Algorithmic Trading Strategy</h2>

<div class="project-tags">
<span>Algorithmic Trading</span>
<span>Momentum Strategy</span>
<span>QuantConnect</span>
<span>Risk Management</span>
<span>Python</span>
</div>

<p>
This project develops a systematic momentum-rotation trading strategy in <strong>QuantConnect</strong> using selected S&P 500 stocks. I combined technical indicators including <strong>SMA</strong>, <strong>RSI</strong>, <strong>Bollinger Bands</strong>, and <strong>20-day momentum</strong> signals to identify long opportunities and construct a rules-based trading framework.
</p>

<p>
The strategy design included both <strong>universe selection</strong> and <strong>risk-management rules</strong>. I screened candidate stocks based on liquidity, sector diversification, recent momentum, volatility, and maximum drawdown, and selected <strong>PLTR</strong>, <strong>GEV</strong>, and <strong>TSLA</strong> as the final trading universe. I also implemented weekly rebalancing, a SPY 200-day market regime filter, fixed and trailing stop-loss rules, trend deterioration exits, and RSI-based exits.
</p>

<p>
To reduce look-ahead bias, I split the backtest into training, validation, and out-of-sample periods. The final out-of-sample backtest achieved a <strong>Sharpe ratio of 2.538</strong> and a <strong>net profit of $2.77M</strong> on a $10M initial portfolio.
</p>

<p><strong>Methods:</strong> momentum rotation, technical indicators, universe selection, backtesting, risk management</p>

<p><strong>Tools:</strong> Python, QuantConnect, pandas</p>

</div>

</div>
